text:
Admission Date:  2007-10-03     Discharge Date:  1996-07-14

Date of Birth:   1993-08-25     Sex:  F


HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  This is a 48-year-old white
female who presents on referral from Cogdell Memorial Hospital in
60163 Flores Mission
Jamestown, WI 92364-7249, [**State 350**] with a 5-day history of severe
headache of acute onset while sitting at his kitchen table
worse headache of life" with intensity of 06-28 pain with
associated photophobia, slight dizziness, mild nausea, and
gradual onset over several minutes of a sense of tingling in
all four extremities.  The patient reports she went to bed,
and the headache persisted and she the onset of vomiting
times one.  The patient reports she called a neighbor for a
ride to the Emergency Room and this led to emergency medical
also complaining of chest pains and anxiety, and the patient
states she was told by the emergency medical technicians that
she was having a panic attack and therefore was not taken to
the Emergency Room.  She therefore remained at home with
headache and nausea with vomiting for approximately two to
three more days until the morning of admission when she went
to the local Emergency Room at Mclaren Orthopedic Hospital where a CT scan was
reportedly negative, with a lumbar puncture with
xanthochromic fluid cerebrospinal fluid with tube #1 showing
15,768 red blood cells and [**Pager number **] white blood cells, and was
reported as pink an hazy, and tube #3 showed 20,340 red blood
cells, 100 white blood cells, and was also reported as pink
and hazy.  Gram stain of the cerebrospinal fluid showed
moderate white blood cells, many red blood cells, with no
organisms, and she was transferred to the Memorial Hermann Southwest Hospital and accepted by Dr. Thompson to the
Neurosurgery Service.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  (Previous medical history includes)
1.  Two pregnancies with two living children, ages
30-year-old female and age 24-year-old male; both are well.
She experienced menopause approximately two years prior to
admission and is status post tubal ligation.
2.  Past history of migraine headaches, but none in the past
two years, and she currently has been out of work for three
months secondary to back pain.

FAMILY HISTORY:  Family history is positive for history of
cerebral aneurysm in six paternal relatives, five of whom
have died secondary to cerebral aneurysm rupture in the
fourth decade of their lives, and the patient's sister is now
10 years status post craniotomy for a clipping of aneurysm at
age middle 30s.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  She smokes one and a half packs per day
times 15 years and reports drinking an occasional beer, but
no more than three to four beers per week.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  Medications include Vioxx and
Flexeril, but she has taken none of these in the past five
days.

ALLERGIES:  She has an allergic reaction to FIORINAL for
which she develops dizziness.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  She is a divorced mother of two adult
children; daughter age 30 lives with the patient.  The
patient had worked as a manager of a Friendly's restaurant
until three months prior to admission.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON ADMISSION:  Vital signs were
temperature of 98.8, pulse rate 85, blood pressure 170/96,
respiratory rate 16, and oxygen saturation was 98% on room
air.  She was pleasant, awake, alert, oriented times three,
and conversant/cooperative white female in minimal distress
secondary to mild headache and mild photophobia.  The entire
general physical examination including the head, ears, nose,
eyes and throat, heart, lungs, and abdomen was essentially
unremarkable.  The extremities showed no clubbing, cyanosis
or edema.  Neurologic examination, again showed the patient
to be awake, fluent speech with no paraphrasic errors, smile
was equal bilaterally, tongue was midline.  There was no
drift of the upper extremities, and the extremities showed
full range of motion, and strength was 03-10 in all groups.
Sensory examination was intact to light touch.  Deep tendon
reflexes were 2+ throughout with plantar response downgoing
bilaterally.  There was no clonus.  The gait examination was
deferred.

LABORATORY DATA ON ADMISSION:   Laboratories at 7 p.m. showed
a white blood cell count of 13.1, hematocrit 36.4, platelet
count of 280.  Coagulations showed PT 11.8, PTT 22.7, and INR
of 0.9.  Sodium 140, potassium 3.7, chloride 103,
bicarbonate 26, BUN 6, creatinine 0.6, and blood sugar 101.

IMPRESSION:  The impression at the time of admission was a
high probability of acute subarachnoid hemorrhage given the
family history and a presentation most consistent with
aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation.

HOSPITAL COURSE:   Therefore, the patient was admitted to the
hospital and seen by Dr. Thompson, and following that visit the
patient was taken to the angiography suite for a diagnostic
angiogram on 2012-06-09.  The diagnostic angiogram
showed a basilar tip aneurysm for which the patient underwent
coiling procedure.  She also was noted to have a right
internal carotid artery bifurcation aneurysm and a left posterior
communicating artery aneurysm, and an anterior communicating
artery aneurysm. The patient tolerated the endovascular procedure
well and went to the Neurosurgical Intensive Care Unit in stable
condition thereafter.  She remained in the Neurosurgical
Intensive Care Unit for several days with "Triple-H therapy"
which included hemodilution to create mild hypertension, and the
patient was maintained on medication to keep the blood pressure
slightly elevated to counteract cerebral vasospasm. She was
maintained on albumin as well and large volumes of intravenous
fluid.

On 06-21, the patient was taken back to angiography
for a repeat diagnostic angiogram which showed no evidence of
continued vasospasm, and the patient tolerated the procedure
well and was returned to the Neurosurgical Intensive Care
Unit in stable condition.  Late that evening, the patient
developed a febrile episode.  A fever workup was obtained,
and cultures of blood and urine were negative.  However, it
was noted that the patient had a small abscess at the site of
a previous central line in the right side of the neck, and
this was incised and drained by Dr. Alexander of the
Neurosurgical Intensive Care Unit attending staff.  The
patient tolerated that procedure quite well, and the
remainder of her postoperative course was essentially
unremarkable.

She was seen in consultation on 08-27 by the Psychiatry
Service for evaluation of insomnia, and recommendations were
made for the patient to have some psychological followup as
an outpatient, and the remainder of her hospitalization was
otherwise unremarkable.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:  She was transferred from the
Neurosurgical Intensive Care Unit to the floor on 09-21,
and subsequently discharged home on 09-04 in stable and
improved condition.  Arrangements were made for followup with
Dr. Thompson in the clinic in approximately two weeks' time, and
a discussion will be held at that time regarding future
elective clipping of the remaining unsecured aneurysms.




                          Brandi Kayla Pena, M.D.  53180

Dictated By:William Henderson

MEDQUIST36

D:  1988-10-02  22:00
T:  1978-01-11  13:04
